entries.title: SГЈo TomГ© and PrГӯncipe heads to polls in tense presidential election
entries.summary: Incumbent Carlos Vila Nova hopes to defeat his former party and secure second term as independentVoters in SГЈo TomГ© and PrГӯncipe go to the polls for a presidential election on Sunday as one of AfricaвҖҷs least populous countries seeks to burnish its democratic credentials.According to the National Election Commission, about 142,000 people are registered to vote in the tiny African stateвҖҷs elections,
entries.content:
Voters in SГЈo TomГ© and PrГӯncipe go to the polls for a presidential election on Sunday as one of AfricaвҖҷs least populous countries seeks to burnish its democratic credentials.

According to the National Election Commission, about 142,000 people are registered to vote in the tiny African stateвҖҷs elections, approximately 15% of whom live in the diaspora.

Since gaining independence from Portugal in 1975, the island country off AfricaвҖҷs west coast has built a solid reputation for peaceful, competitive elections. But this yearвҖҷs vote comes amid a tense political atmosphere and a lingering constitutional crisis.

The president, Carlos Vila Nova, is running for a second term as an independent, rather than as the candidate for the ruling AcГ§ГЈo DemocrГЎtica Independente (ADI), whom he represented when he was elected in 2021.

The tensions between Vila Nova and his former party began in January last year when he dismissed the prime minister, Patrice Trovoada. TrovoadaвҖҷs replacement, the former justice minister Ilza Amado Vaz, resigned after just three days, before the current holder, AmГ©rico Ramos, took office.

On Sunday, Vila Nova will be running against four other candidates including the ADI parliamentary leader, Nito DвҖҷAbreu. Another former prime minister, Jorge Bom Jesus, attempted to withdraw his independent candidacy, but missed the deadline and remains on the ballot.

The main opposition party, the Movement for the Liberation of SГЈo TomГ© and PrГӯncipe, is now part of a broad coalition backing the incumbent president, despite being staunch ADI opponents. Meanwhile, an ADI faction led by Ramos is backing DвҖҷAbreu. If no candidate receives more than 50% of the vote, the election will go on to a second round.

The businessman Domingos Monteiro, known as вҖңNinoвҖқ, who has been president of the SГЈo TomГ©an Football Federation since 2015, was disqualified after the constitutional court ruled he did not meet the eligibility requirements. Monteiro was born on the island, but his parents never became naturalised citizens after moving from Cape Verde where they were born.

вҖңThe constitutional court is violating the principle of equal rights вҖҰ It is time for descendants born in SГЈo TomГ© and PrГӯncipe to say no to discrimination, to the culture of xenophobia and persecution,вҖқ said Monteiro.

For voters, priorities include tackling government corruption, high inflation, severe youth unemployment, chronic fuel shortages, and frequent blackouts. Rivals of Vila Nova are hoping this will keep him from a second term and propel them to the presidency.

Corruption in SГЈo TomГ© and PrГӯncipe вҖңis the greatest catastrophe the country has experienced in this half-century [of independenceвҖқ, DвҖҷAbreu said at the presidential debate on Tuesday.

The country is seen as a trusted western ally for security and maritime trade in the Gulf of Guinea, one of the worldвҖҷs biggest maritime piracy hotspots. It also has offshore oil deposits that make a significant contribution to its economy. Observer missions from the European Union, G7+ and the Community of Portuguese Language Countries are on the ground to monitor the polls.

entries.title: вҖҳWe are preserving a traditionвҖҷ: how GhanaвҖҷs sensationalist film posters became collectible art
entries.summary: Hand-painted works are often wildly unfaithful to the movies they portray вҖ“ reinterpretations that sometimes resulted in threats, insults and even physical attacks from viewers who felt dupedSitting on his porch in Teshie near Accra, Heavy J dipped a brush into red oil paint and dabbed it carefully on to his canvas вҖ“ a flour sack вҖ“ adding blood to a knife being wielded by a man. Higher on the canv
entries.content:
Sitting on his porch in Teshie near Accra, Heavy J dipped a brush into red oil paint and dabbed it carefully on to his canvas вҖ“ a flour sack вҖ“ adding blood to a knife being wielded by a man. Higher on the canvas, he had started on an outline of a skull.

Heavy J was creating a poster, but not as you might have expected for a horror film. Instead, it was for the animated fairytale The Little Mermaid. The man with the knife was not a killer but the filmвҖҷs kind-hearted prince, Eric. The skull was also unrelated to the story. вҖңWe add more to make people interested,вҖқ said Heavy J, whose real name is Jeaurs Affutu.

Hand-painted film posters by local artists were a hallmark of Ghanaian film culture from the late 1970s to the early 2000s, used to advertise screenings for neighbourhood venues known as video clubs after organisers realised that the original posters were not attracting enough audiences.

Plot lines were regarded as little more than jump-off points for humorous and surreal flights of fancy. Artists working for different video clubs competed to make the best вҖңforgeryвҖқ, as they described their interpretations.

The practice began to wane around the turn of the century as more Ghanaians gained access to electricity and their own TV sets and video players. Many video clubs went out of business and painters pivoted to create other work. But by then the posters had attained global interest, popularised in books and foreign exhibitions, and old and rare paintings became prized collectibles.

There was a lull in interest in newly painted posters in the early part of the 21st century, but demand has risen, driven by online marketing and a receptive customer base of film lovers in the west.

Deadly Prey Gallery has been working with artists to preserve the culture of making hand-painted film posters, while helping meet the increased demand.

Named after an action film, the business was co-founded in 2012 by Brian Chankin, then a video rental store owner in Chicago, and Robert Kofi, a Ghanaian who, as a child, used to work as a вҖңhype manвҖқ for video centres in his home town of Winneba and later started collecting and selling posters. They partnered after Kofi sold Chankin some works.

Deadly Prey Gallery works with 15 artists, including Heavy J, who has been painting posters for four decades, connecting them to online customers and shipping the artwork on completion.

Most orders come from the US. Old action, science fiction and horror films have the highest demand. Popular titles include The Exorcist and the Star Wars and Terminator franchises. And prices for commissioned pieces start at $600 (ВЈ450) and vary based on the canvas size and other customer preferences.

Kofi, who is based in Accra, is part manager and part editor. He identifies the artists most suited for each work, shares his vision of the posters with them and makes regular visits to their workspaces to review works in progress.

In a studio in Ashaiman, about 11 miles from Heavy JвҖҷs house, another artist called Stoger was working on two commissions: one for the horror film Poltergeist, and another for the experimental drama Gummo, which contains multiple acts of violence against cats and a scene in which a character eats spaghetti in a bath tub.

The poster showed three cats and a man in a bath with a plate of spaghetti in front of him. In his feedback that day, Kofi, speaking in Ga, a primary language of Ghana, told Stoger two of the cats were not aggressive enough and the food was too clean. вҖңI want uglier cat scenes,вҖқ he later explained. вҖңThe spaghetti has to be dirtier.вҖқ

Stoger, born Benjamin Amartey, was a sculptor before developing an interest in films and becoming a poster painter in 1992. вҖңI use my imagination to make scenes that will attract people so that theyвҖҷll love the poster,вҖқ he said.

The emphasis on exaggeration comes from an African tradition of вҖңvisualising the invisibleвҖқ, said Joseph Oduro-Frimpong, the director of the Centre of African Popular Culture at Ashesi University and a poster collector himself.

вҖңThe postersвҖҷ audiences have not seen the film, so itвҖҷs impossible for them to know [whether they are accurate]. Therefore, the artists tap into what they call imaginative painting,вҖқ he said. вҖңThey will highlight these things and in doing so they incorporate things that are not in there. There is a kind of sensationalism to it.вҖқ

The reinterpretations at times resulted in threats, insults and even physical attacks from viewers who felt duped. Kofi laughed when he recalled an incident in the 1990s when people beat him up after they watched the action film Double Impact and realised it did not have a scene showing Jean-Claude Van Damme carrying out a beheading, as was illustrated on the poster.

At the Centre for National Culture in Accra, dozens of colourful posters from Deadly Prey Gallery are pinned on wooden walls. They include Jennifer Lopez launching an arrow at a snake in Anaconda, and a mouse coming out of Jamie Lee CurtisвҖҷ mouth in Halloween.

вҖңWe are preserving a tradition,вҖқ Kofi said of the poster-painting craft. вҖңWe are preserving a history.вҖқ

entries.title: Seven Americans quarantining at Kenya Ebola facility after US travel ban, says aid group
entries.summary: Aid workers are first known people to quarantine at facility, which sparked huge opposition in Kenya Seven American aid workers who had been in Congo to fight the Ebola outbreak are quarantining at a new isolation facility in вҒ Kenya after the US government introduced travel вҒ restrictions, the head of a US charity вҖӢemploying them told Reuters.The aid workers are the first known people to quarantine
entries.content:
Seven American aid workers who had been in Congo to fight the Ebola outbreak are quarantining at a new isolation facility in вҒ Kenya after the US government introduced travel вҒ restrictions, the head of a US charity вҖӢemploying them told Reuters.

The aid workers are the first known people to quarantine at the facility, which has sparked huge opposition in Kenya and is at the heart of a legal case in which a court has ordered the work to be вҒ suspended. Construction continued, however, according to US officials and satellite imagery reviewed by Reuters.

WashingtonвҖҷs new policy says American citizens returning from the Democratic Republic of the Congo, where there is an Ebola outbreak, must spend three weeks in a third country before entering the United States.

The US government is building вҒ the 50-bed bio-isolation unit on an air force base in central Kenya for asymptomatic Americans exposed to the virus in Democratic Republic of the Congo or Uganda. Many Kenyans are angered at what вҖӢthey see as the US offloading the health risk such patients pose.

Last month, KenyaвҖҷs вҖҢhealth minister announced an immediate halt to the facilityвҖҷs вҖҢconstruction after he was found in contempt of court for failing to observe the order to halt work pending a final ruling.

вҖңSamaritanвҖҷs Purse has seven American Disaster Assistance Response Team staff вҖҢmembers there,вҖқ Franklin Graham, president and CEO of SamaritanвҖҷs Purse, told Reuters in response to questions.

вҖңNone of them have any symptoms, but they are being quarantined by the Kenyan government for 21 days,вҖқ Graham said.

A US state department official told Reuters a group of asymptomatic Americans who had served on the frontlines of the Ebola response had вҖңvoluntarily moved to the Kenya facility for precautionary monitoring and isolationвҖқ.

вҖңKenyan authorities have authorized their movement into the facility under the observation of the US public health service clinicians,вҖқ the official said, adding that the decision was taken вҖңstrictly out of an abundance of cautionвҖқ.

Kenyan health ministry officials did not immediately respond to calls or requests for comment вҖҢon the move. A senior Kenyan foreign ministry official said they had no information on it.

Another source familiar with the matter who asked for anonymity said that the group had arrived at the site in central Kenya on Monday and were sleeping in army cots in tents.

He said some вҖӢhad treated Ebola patients at the Christian aid groupвҖҷs treatment centres in Congo but others had carried out work such as construction, with no direct contact with the sick.

вҖңThere is one potential high-risk exposure,вҖқ he said, adding that their health was being monitored. Kenyan authorities are not allowing the group to leave the facility to travel anywhere else in the country, he added.

The often fatal viral disease spreads through direct contact with bodily fluids from infected people or animals.

SamaritanвҖҷs Purse, which has been promised several million dollars from the Trump administration for the вҒ Ebola response, is an evangelical Christian group that works in disaster zones around the world.

It is one of the biggest foreign вҖӢaid groups treating Ebola in Congo and has the largest number of вҖӢAmericans there, working closely alongside the World Health Organization to вҖӢcontain the outbreak. One of its US staff members who caught Ebola earlier this month was transferred to a hospital in Germany on Monday.

entries.title: UK aid cuts вҖҳreduce bilateral support to some African countries by 90%вҖҷ
entries.summary: Critics say Foreign Office figures send вҖҳglobal message about the role the country wants to play on international stageвҖҷLabourвҖҷs foreign aid cuts mean reductions of as much as 90% in the bilateral support the UK will give to some African countries, Foreign Office figures show.The departmentвҖҷs annual report includes a long-awaited breakdown of how the reduction in the aid budget will affect individ
entries.content:
LabourвҖҷs foreign aid cuts mean reductions of as much as 90% in the bilateral support the UK will give to some African countries, Foreign Office figures show.

The departmentвҖҷs annual report includes a long-awaited breakdown of how the reduction in the aid budget will affect individual countries for the next three years.

Analysis by Bond, the umbrella group for development charities, shows cuts of 90% for Mozambique and Malawi by 2029, 80% for Rwanda and Sierra Leone and 49% for Somalia.

BondвҖҷs chief executive, Romilly Greenhill, said: вҖңBy slashing UK aid funding to countries like Ethiopia, Malawi, Mozambique, Rwanda, Sierra Leone and Uganda, this Labour government is abandoning communities on the frontlines of conflict and the climate crisis and risks plunging these countriesвҖҷ populations into poverty and instability.вҖқ

Keir StarmerвҖҷs government announced deep reductions to spending on overseas aid last year to fund increases in the defence budget, prompting the resignation of Anneliese Dodds as development minister.

Part of LabourвҖҷs approach in implementing the cuts has been to switch its focus to funding multilateral donors such as the World Bank, which it argues is a more efficient use of straitened resources.

Explaining the cuts in a written statement to parliament in March, the foreign secretary, Yvette Cooper, said: вҖңIn a range of countries, we will transition away from spending high levels of grant ODA [overseas development assistance], but our ambition and effort will remain high вҖ“ delivering through modernised partnerships, and making the most of what the UK has to offer.вҖқ

Charities, however, say the scale of the reduction in direct support to individual countries will jeopardise vital projects.

The director of global outcomes at Save the Children, Lisa Wise, said: вҖңTodayвҖҷs international budget allocations reflect what we already know вҖ“ reductions in public investment in countries and children that need it most. These choices send a global message about the role the UK wants to play on the international stage.вҖқ

The next steps for LabourвҖҷs development policy will fall to the incoming prime minister Andy BurnhamвҖҷs pick for foreign secretary. The current energy secretary, Ed Miliband, is seen as a likely contender for the role.

Some MPs have urged Burnham to act to restore the partyвҖҷs leadership on development, including by setting a path back towards the target of spending 0.7% of national income on overseas aid.

The UK takes on the chair of the G20 next year вҖ“ the coordinating body that includes China, India and Brazil alongside wealthy countries in the global north.

Greenhill urged the new PM and foreign secretary to use that role вҖңto champion the global reforms needed to address poverty and inequality among the worldвҖҷs marginalised communitiesвҖқ.

The development minister, Jenny Chapman, said: вҖңThe world has changed. Crises in one part of the world now affect us all.

вҖңJust this year, conflict in the Middle East has driven up food and fertiliser costs and the Ebola outbreak that began in the DRC is an urgent reminder why global health security matters.

вҖңWeвҖҷre not turning away from these challenges. WeвҖҷre making every pound of UK development spending work harder, for people facing the toughest crises and for taxpayers at home.вҖқ

entries.title: Uganda calls for travel restrictions to be lifted after last Ebola patient discharged
entries.summary: Country begins 42-day countdown to outbreak being declared officially over, as numbers continue to rise in neighbouring Democratic Republic of the CongoUganda has started lobbying countries to lift Ebola-related travel restrictions after discharging its last confirmed Ebola patient from hospital.The discharge of a Congolese national from the Mulago national referral hospitalвҖҷs isolation centre in
entries.content:
Uganda has started lobbying countries to lift Ebola-related travel restrictions after discharging its last confirmed Ebola patient from hospital.

The discharge of a Congolese national from the Mulago national referral hospitalвҖҷs isolation centre in Kampala on Thursday triggered the start of a 42-day countdown required by the World Health Organization before Uganda can officially be declared Ebola-free, provided no new infections are detected.

The outbreak in the country infected 20 people and claimed two lives.

Fifteen countries continue to maintain either partial or full travel restrictions on Uganda after the outbreak, measures the government said have crippled tourism, trade and business, despite the countryвҖҷs relatively successful response.

The US has placed Uganda on a level four advisory against all travel due to the Ebola outbreak. The same category includes countries such as North Korea, Somalia, Afghanistan and Russia.

вҖңAs we make progress in managing this disease, we are engaging and asking those countries with a view to opening up so that the economy does not get injured,вҖқ UgandaвҖҷs health minister, Dr Chris Baryomunsi, said after the ceremony marking the release of the last patient.

Baryomunsi said the country was not yet Ebola-free вҖ“ under WHO guidelines, countries must complete two consecutive 21-day incubation periods without recording a new case before an outbreak can officially be declared over.

вҖңThis is an imported outbreak. We remain on high alert because what we are celebrating here is different from what is happening in the Democratic Republic of the Congo,вҖқ he said.

The DRC reported вҒ on Thursday that вҒ the вҖӢnumber вҖҢof вҖҢconfirmed вҖҢcases in the country had вҖҢrisen to вҖӢ2,073, including вҒ 796 вҖӢdeaths, as вҖӢof вҖӢ14 July. The outbreak, caused by the Bundibugyo strain of the virus, was first declared by the WHO on 17 May.

UgandaвҖҷs outbreak has been markedly different from previous Ebola emergencies in the region, with only two deaths and 20 confirmed infections including 15 Congolese nationals, four health workers and one driver.

WHOвҖҷs representative in Uganda, Dr Kasonde Mwinga, said the countryвҖҷs response demonstrated the value of sustained investment in epidemic preparedness.

вҖңThe case fatality rate of less than 10% is among the lowest recorded in Ebola outbreaks because the lowest has always been 30%. That is not by luck. It is because people invested in preparedness,вҖқ she said.

Mwinga said Uganda had established treatment facilities, trained emergency medical teams and pre-positioned medical supplies before the outbreak, allowing authorities to respond rapidly when cases were detected.

The outbreak also accelerated scientific efforts to develop vaccines against the Bundibugyo strain of Ebola, for which no licensed vaccine exists.

Dr Ronnie Bahatungire, the clinical services commissioner at UgandaвҖҷs health ministry, said the country had already participated in trials involving experimental therapies during the outbreak and plans to join vaccine studies. вҖңWe will participate at whatever point in time when an opportunity arises,вҖқ Bahatungire said.

This week, Oxford UniversityвҖҷs Oxford Vaccine Group launched the worldвҖҷs first Phase I clinical trial of BD-Ebov, a vaccine candidate targeting the Bundibugyo strain, in response to the outbreak, with the first patient already enrolled.

Despite UgandaвҖҷs progress, authorities said the threat remains across the border. Baryomunsi said presidents Yoweri Museveni of Uganda and FГ©lix Tshisekedi of DRC had agreed on closer cross-border cooperation and signed a bilateral memorandum of understanding, where Uganda has already sent health experts to DRC to contain the outbreak and stop Congolese travelling to Uganda to seek medical attention.

вҖңWe have so far sent 50 health workers with four laboratories and these have been set up in four different locations. We agreed that if they require additional human resources, we shall send more depending on how the situation progresses,вҖқ Baryomunsi said.

entries.title: Mexican drug lord вҖҳEl MayoвҖҷ sentenced to life in prison in US federal court
entries.summary: Case against Ismael Zambada GarcГӯa, who helped co-found and lead the violent Sinaloa cartel, unfolded in New YorkThe Mexican drug kingpin, Ismael Zambada GarcГӯa, known as вҖңEl MayoвҖқ, was sentenced in a US federal court on Monday to life in prison, after a plea deal last year in which he admitted вҒ to shipping vast amounts of cocaine and other substances as leader of the violent Sinaloa cartel.Zambad
entries.content:
The Mexican drug kingpin, Ismael Zambada GarcГӯa, known as вҖңEl MayoвҖқ, was sentenced in a US federal court on Monday to life in prison, after a plea deal last year in which he admitted вҒ to shipping vast amounts of cocaine and other substances as leader of the violent Sinaloa cartel.

Zambada is 76 and one of the most high-profile Mexican вҒ drug traffickers to be sentenced in a US court since his fellow Sinaloa leader JoaquГӯn вҖңEl ChapoвҖқ GuzmГЎn вҖӢwas sentenced to life in prison in вҖҢ2019. The case unfolded in federal court in Brooklyn, New York.

Zambada helped co-found and lead the cartel, becoming one of the most wanted targets by US intelligence and law enforcement agencies for decades.

He signed a plea deal last August, admitting charges including racketeering conspiracy and continuing criminal enterprise, after the вҖҢUS justice department said it would not seek the death penalty. The court also ordered him to forfeit $15bn from drug trade profits as part of his punishment.

As one of the co-founders of the Sinaloa cartel, El Mayo is one of the biggest drug traffickers in the history of the US-Mexico drug war. He was accused of trafficking tons of cocaine, marijuana, heroin and, in recent years, fentanyl to the US and other parts of the world.

Prosecutors also accused him of being one of the leaders of a violent organization that led to corruption among Mexican politicians, military officials and law enforcement agents.

Zambada, who has spent nearly two years inside a Brooklyn federal prison, limped inside the courtroom. He was wearing a beige prison outfit and had white hair and a beard.

Before his sentencing, Zambada read a public letter, saying that he took responsibility for his crimes and calling for an end to the violence that has plagued Mexico for decades during the drug-fueled conflict.

вҖңI did not go to trial because I did not want to deny the truth,вҖқ Zambada read aloud in court, adding that вҖңviolence must endвҖқ and urging the next generation of drug traffickers to вҖңchoose a different pathвҖқ.

While El Chapo is better known as the face of the Sinaloa cartel, the elderly El Mayo was regarded as the elusive, behind the scenes leader running the operations, with a low profile and few public sightings.

El MayoвҖҷs arrest in the US in 2024 came as a surprise. During a mysterious operation, he was known to have been beaten and kidnapped by one of El ChapoвҖҷs sons, JoaquГӯn GuzmГЎn LГіpez, and was flown across the US border to New Mexico, where he was arrested by US federal agents.

It remains unknown whether US agencies helped facilitate the cross-border kidnapping operation that led to his arrest. GuzmГЎn LГіpez pled guilty in an abduction case late last year and has become a cooperating witness for the US justice department.

El MayoвҖҷs arrest led to a splintering of the Sinaloa cartel, with El MayoвҖҷs son and El ChapoвҖҷs sons leading different factions, in a raging intra-cartel battle for territory that has ravaged parts of Mexico.

El MayoвҖҷs sentencing comes at a tense time in US-Mexico relations, as the Trump administration has escalated its pressure on the Mexican government to crack down harder on criminal groups. Last year, the Trump administration designated the Sinaloa cartel as a terrorist group and has threatened US military intervention to attack the cartel and other drug-trafficking organizations.

US district judge Brian Cogan, who also presided over El ChapoвҖҷs trial, has been hearing the case in New York.

Cogan acknowledged that the sentencing hearing was вҖңanticlimacticвҖқ because of the plea agreement. All parties involved understood that Zambada would spend the rest of his life in prison, the judge said.

Frank Perez, a lawyer for El Mayo, вҖӢhad said in a 6 July court filing that his client was fully aware he would receive a life sentence when he decided to accept responsibility for his crimes. Perez asked Cogan to recommend to the US Bureau of Prisons that his client, as a septuagenarian, be placed in a вҒ facility where he would receive adequate medical care.

Prosecutors with the Brooklyn US attorneyвҖҷs office said the prisons вҖӢsuggested by Perez may not вҖӢbe equipped to handle the security concerns posed by Zambada, including the вҖӢpossibility that he could вҖҢcontinue to direct вҖӢcartel activities from вҖӢbehind bars.

вҖңThose security concerns will continue for the foreseeable future because individuals loyal to the defendant continue to control the cartel,вҖқ the prosecutors wrote in a 13 July filing.

ZambadaвҖҷs defense attorneys on Monday requested that the judge recommend a federal medical facility, rather than a traditional prison, due to his medical conditions. Health details are redacted in court records, but the judge mentioned during the sentencing hearing that Zambada is вҖңdriftingвҖқ towards being вҖңcognitively impairedвҖқ.

Mexican outlets have reported Zambada is dealing with the early stages of dementia. The Bureau of Prisons still has to evaluate where Zambada will be sent to serve the rest of his days.

El Chapo is serving his life sentence at a maximum-security prison in Colorado.

Frank Perez, one of ZambadaвҖҷs attorneys, said during the sentencing hearing that his client did not cooperate with the US government, neither providing information to US officials about other cartel members or leaders, nor Mexican officials who have protected the cartelвҖҷs drug-trafficking business.

Reuters contributed reporting

entries.title: Canada issues air quality warnings over US wildfire smoke after Trump tariff threat
entries.summary: Officials say smoke from US fires has crossed border days after US president accused Canada of improper fire managementAfter days of acrid smoke from northern wildfires choking major US cities and inspiring Donald Trump to threaten Canada with tariffs, officials now say the reverse is also happening as parts of Canada issue air quality warnings caused by American fires to the south.Officials in th
entries.content:
After days of acrid smoke from northern wildfires choking major US cities and inspiring Donald Trump to threaten Canada with tariffs, officials now say the reverse is also happening as parts of Canada issue air quality warnings caused by American fires to the south.

Officials in the Canadian province of British Columbia warned in an alert on Saturday evening that smoke from US fires, spurred on by thousands of lightning strikes in the north-east, was creating smoky conditions as polluted air drifted north.

вҖңMuch of this smoke is originating from fires south of the Canada-US border in Washington and Oregon,вҖқ the provincial wildfire service said.

Twenty-two large fires are burning across Washington and Oregon, according to the Northwest Interagency Coordination Center, the fire coordination agency for both states. British Columbia has air quality warnings in place for two regions, Kootenay Lake and Cranbrook, located along the south-east border with the US.

Despite the US facing its own wildfire issues, Donald Trump and his fellow Republicans have chosen to accuse Canada of improper wildfire management and have threatened to hit Canadian officials with sanctions.

In a social media post on Friday, Trump wrote the US had been вҖңinvadedвҖқ by dirty air. вҖң[The] вҖҢcost of this pollution must of necessity be added вҒ to the TARIFFS Canada is currently paying,вҖқ he said. The Trump administration has taken a sledgehammer to several climate protections.

Wildfires continue to blaze across the US and Canada. According to the Canadian government, as of Monday there were 843 active wildfires burning across Canada. Of those 181 are considered вҖңout of controlвҖқ, meaning the fires are spreading and not responding to suppressive actions. Another 532 of those fires are also considered вҖңout of controlвҖқ but suppressive actions have not yet been taken.

The Canadian prime minister, Mark Carney, who sat alongside Trump at the World Cup final on Sunday, where the US president again suggested slapping Canada with tariffs, has sidestepped the criticisms. вҖңFighting climate change is the responsibility of all countries, including the United States,вҖқ he told reporters last week.

But Doug Ford, the premier of Ontario, where many fires are burning, said on Friday: вҖңIf thereвҖҷs politicians out there chirping away, well maybe what you should do rather than complain is send support, send help, because we have done the exact same thing for our American friends.вҖқ

Eleanor Olszewski, CanadaвҖҷs minister of emergency preparedness, said in a statement that Canada had invested C$12bn (ВЈ6.36bn) on fire prevention and forest sustainability over the past five years. вҖңThis is a challenge that knows no borders,вҖқ she said.

entries.title: вҖҳItвҖҷs total devastationвҖҷ: inside the Amazonian war against illegal gold mining
entries.summary: The Guardian goes on the ground with Brazilian forces fighting to eradicate mining that has destroyed protected areas of Indigenous landThe jerry-built bordello at the foot of Vila Cururu, an illegal gold-mining settlement at the heart of BrazilвҖҷs most desecrated Indigenous territory, was a hive of depravity until just a few weeks ago.Inebriated prospectors huddled around wooden tables, swigging b
entries.content:
The jerry-built bordello at the foot of Vila Cururu, an illegal gold-mining settlement at the heart of BrazilвҖҷs most desecrated Indigenous territory, was a hive of depravity until just a few weeks ago.

Inebriated prospectors huddled around wooden tables, swigging beer and Scotch whisky, and ogling pole-dancing sex workers before going with them to shacks behind the brothel.

The encircling encampment also teemed with intruders as hordes of miners used pickaxes and explosives to burrow deep into the hillsides and savanna in search of treasure.

вҖңThey wiped out everything. They destroyed nature,вҖқ said Jackson KatitГЈurlu, an Indigenous leader from the Nambiquara people, whose village is a few miles from the chaos in the south-western Amazon.

One recent morning, however, Vila Cururu was deserted: the once-bustling illicit community having been razed by Brazilian security forces as they fought to fulfil a pledge by the president, Luiz InГЎcio Lula da Silva, to eradicate illegal mining in Indigenous territories after years of mayhem under his far-right predecessor, Jair Bolsonaro.

As LulaвҖҷs eviction tsar, Nilton Tubino, hiked through the encampmentвҖҷs charred ruins, he recalled how miners fled into the surrounding forests after troops launched a land and air assault in late March to halt one of the most ferocious attacks on Indigenous lands in BrazilвҖҷs recent history.

Other much larger protected territories have suffered damage on a greater physical scale but nowhere was the vandalism as intense as in SararГ©, a Singapore-sized chunk of savanna and woodland near the border with Bolivia that lost 4% of its forests between January 2024 and August 2025. A total of about 17.3 sq miles (44.8km) have been destroyed since the gold rush began about five years ago вҖ“ an area almost half the size of Manhattan.

вҖңItвҖҷs total devastation,вҖқ Tubino sighed as he marched through Garimpo Quatro, one of SararГ©вҖҷs largest mines, with an 18-inch machete strung across the front of his black fatigues and three radios strapped to his belt.

Paradoxically, part of the explanation for SararГ©вҖҷs ordeal lies in the success of LulaвҖҷs anti-mining crusade, which he launched after returning to power in January 2023. After TubinoвҖҷs team kickstarted LulaвҖҷs вҖңdisintrusionвҖқ drive in the better-known Yanomami Indigenous territory, some miners flocked south to SararГ©, hoping to continue working far from the crackdown.

Another driving force has been surging gold prices, which have hit record levels amid the global instability unleashed by Donald Trump and his erratic foreign policy. вҖңThe price of gold, this crazy global context, Trump, these things вҖҰ [have a direct impact] all across the Amazon,вҖқ said Tubino, who saw a clear link between global turmoil and the annihilation of the forests around him, as investors sought a safe haven for their money.

The Guardian spent two days touring the destruction in SararГ© with government forces, the only foreign media organisation given permission to visit the embattled territory, where about 200 members of the Nambiquara people live.

Seen from a helicopter, the astonishing extent of the depredation became clear. Seemingly endless brown gashes had been ripped across the once verdant landscape, dotted with pools of stagnant bronze-coloured water contaminated with the mercury that miners use in their giant sluice boxes.

Above Vila Cururu, which is named after a poisonous tropical toad with a propensity for digging, the pilot gasped as he absorbed the scale of the illegal operation. вҖңItвҖҷs like a favela. ItвҖҷs like Rio de Janeiro,вҖқ he said of the sprawling mining camp as his six-seat aircraft swooped down towards dozens of demolished makeshift structures cobbled together from tarpaulins and planks.

Nearby, half a dozen tunnels had been bored into a rock face in the frenzied hunt for gold.

On a nearby plain, an even larger mine had been gouged out of the forest by Hyundai excavators that the security forces had now torched. The trees had been levelled and replaced by colossal hillocks of grey and copper-coloured rubble over which red macaws soared.

вҖңItвҖҷs a complete desert. I was born and raised here and when I saw it I just couldnвҖҷt believe it,вҖқ said KatitГЈurlu, describing how his territoryвҖҷs rivers were poisoned and its wildlife scared off by the constant din of music and explosions.

The crisis in SararГ©, which began during BolsonaroвҖҷs environmentally catastrophic 2019-2023 presidency, is not the Nambiquara peopleвҖҷs first taste of outside aggression.

In David PriceвҖҷs 1989 book Before the Bulldozer, about the small Indigenous group, the anthropologist described how outsiders first came to the region in the 1730s when gold was found in the surrounding hills. вҖңThe heavy work of digging and washing the gravel was done by slaves, first Indians, and then Africans,вҖқ Price wrote.

More misery came after the Nambiquara were вҖңdiscoveredвҖқ in the early 1900s by the explorer CГўndido Rondon, who had been sent to the border region to build a telegraph line. That mission вҖңbrought new and dreadful diseases to which the Indians had no defence. There were epidemics of flu in the 1920s that decimated large groups in a matter of days. An epidemic of measles at the beginning of 1946 was so devastating that survivors could not bury the dead,вҖқ Price wrote.

There was more disease and violence in the late 1970s, as the military dictatorship bulldozed a large highway through their lands with support from the World Bank. In just a few years, Price wrote, the Nambiquara had gone from being вҖңa free and proud peopleвҖқ to having been вҖңoverwhelmed by western societyвҖқ. Their population had plunged from perhaps 10,000 at the start of the 20th century to about 600.

TodayвҖҷs emergency has two new and disturbing elements: the use of cutting-edge heavy-duty hydraulic excavators, which have enabled devastation on an unprecedented industrial scale, and the suspected arrival of heavily armed organised crime groups such as Rio de JaneiroвҖҷs Red Command.

Drug factions from Rio and SГЈo Paulo have been advancing into the Amazon in recent years and some reports have claimed that the Red Command, which the Trump administration recently designated a foreign terrorist group, had seized control of Vila Cururu and other SararГ© mines, causing an explosion of bloodshed.

Tubino played down the Red CommandвҖҷs possible role in the region and said he had yet to see hard proof it ran SararГ©вҖҷs goldmines. But the growing violence is undeniable. When environmental special forces raided the area in 2024, five heavily armed men were killed in a gunfight. Days earlier four people were murdered during an apparent goldmine turf war.

Saulo KatitГЈurlu, SararГ©вҖҷs top Indigenous leader, said he had heard reports of miners roaming the territory with automatic rifles, 12-gauge shotguns and pistols. He was convinced they were faction members and recalled receiving threats from one miner who warned him not to speak out about the destruction, вҖңotherwise IвҖҷll come to your village and kill the lot of youвҖқ.

Jackson KatitГЈurlu, SauloвҖҷs brother, said that a few days earlier Indigenous hunters had found a corpse near their village, wrapped in a tarpaulin and with a deep slash wound to its chest. When police arrived to recover the body, it had disappeared.

Nambiquara leaders said the crackdown appeared to have largely succeeded, for now, in expelling the miners. But what would happen when troops inevitably left?

вҖңGood question,вҖқ Jackson answered. вҖңIf the operation is suspended, I think theyвҖҷll come back. 100% they will come back.вҖқ

Even more daunting was the prospect of Lula losing this yearвҖҷs presidential election to BolsonaroвҖҷs senator son, FlГЎvio, with the leftist not far ahead in the polls. вҖңIf that happens, weвҖҷre fucked,вҖқ Jackson joked grimly, recalling how BolsonaroвҖҷs anti-Indigenous rhetoric emboldened a historic gold rush during which tens of thousands of miners flooded into protected areas.

Tubino said he believed LulaвҖҷs re-election was crucial if the inter-agency clampdown on illegal mining and deforestation was to continue, safeguarding Indigenous and other traditional communities in the Amazon. He believed BrazilвҖҷs sovereignty was also at stake in OctoberвҖҷs vote, noting how Trump had used NicolГЎs MaduroвҖҷs alleged complicity with illegal mining as one of the pretexts for abducting VenezuelaвҖҷs president in January this year.

For now, though, the vigorous 65-year-old has more immediate concerns.

Despite the crackdown вҖ“ the 10th that Tubino has spearheaded under Lula вҖ“ some trespassers still lurk in SararГ©. As his convoy rolled into Vila Cururu, police radios picked up chatter between lookouts in the hills. вҖңThereвҖҷs a guy in a flak jacket taking photographs and two different-looking guys,вҖқ one scout could be heard announcing, apparently referring to the GuardianвҖҷs reporters.

Further ahead, Tubino crawled into a claustrophobic, pitch-black 100-metre-long tunnel that had been drilled out of an escarpment, and found tools suggesting miners were still at work вҖ“ perhaps operating by night to avoid detection.

Nearby, troops uncovered an underground bunker containing a generator capable of powering the whole of Vila Cururu.

In a patch of undergrowth beside another deactivated mine, troops found white sacks containing 300kg of ammonium nitrate explosives. вҖңItвҖҷs painstaking work,вҖқ Tubino said as troops made that discovery after spotting fresh footprints on a dirt path. вҖңSomeoneвҖҷs walked through here in the past few days,вҖқ he added, before giving orders for the explosives to be destroyed.

LulaвҖҷs anti-mining campaign is back-breaking, dangerous and seemingly interminable work for the men and women on the frontline of the Amazonian war against a multibillion-dollar illegal industry. But with another big mission planned for later this year, Tubino vowed that work would go on.

вҖңHolidays? ThereвҖҷs no such thing as holidays,вҖқ he quipped as he sat in the wreckage of a recently destroyed mining shack, mosquitoes swarming around his black sleeves.

entries.title: Huge search operation after passenger ferry sinks off coast of Guyana
entries.summary: Authorities launch criminal investigation after passenger list вҖ“ showing more than 100 passengers вҖ“ was found to be inaccurate, complicating rescue effortsA massive search-and-rescue operation is under way after a ferry sank вҒ off the coast of Guyana in South America вҒ late on Saturday, in what has been described by officials as one of the countryвҖҷs worst maritime disasters.Authorities launched a cr
entries.content:
A massive search-and-rescue operation is under way after a ferry sank вҒ off the coast of Guyana in South America вҒ late on Saturday, in what has been described by officials as one of the countryвҖҷs worst maritime disasters.

Authorities launched a criminal investigation after a government official said the captain and a crew member of the ferry allegedly tested positive for marijuana and the vesselвҖҷs passenger manifest was found to be inaccurate.

Two people have been confirmed dead in the accident.

Authorities had previously said that there were 116 passengers and 17 crew members on board вҖ“ as stated on the shipвҖҷs manifest вҖ“ and confirmed that there was no mechanical problem and the vessel had been operating within its legal limits.

But speaking at a press conference alongside the countryвҖҷs prime minister late on Sunday, Juan Edghill, GuyanaвҖҷs minister of public works, told reporters the manifest was wrong after it was discovered that only 35 of 67 people rescued so far were on the passenger list.

The captain and the first engineer, both of whom allegedly tested positive for cannabis, are now in police custody.

вҖңI am outraged. We are talking about operational breaches; people will be dealt with,вҖқ Edghill said at a press conference in Georgetown on Sunday night.

The ferry MV Barima left Georgetown, the capital of the English-speaking South American country, on Saturday at about 3.15pm for Port Kaituma, located in the oil-rich Essequibo region and just off the border with Venezuela.

A distress call was received shortly after 11pm, prompting an emergency response involving the Guyana Defence Force Coast Guard and Air Corps, private aircraft, fishing vessels and civilian volunteers.

Edghill had previously said the MV Barima was certified to carry 397 passengers and 284 tonnes of cargo. The ship was equipped with 250 lifejackets, two lifeboats and six inflatable rafts, Edghill had said.

The countryвҖҷs prime minister Mark Phillips described the news that the manifest was incorrect as вҖңdeeply concerning and shockingвҖқ.

вҖңThe government has ordered an urgent and comprehensive investigation involving the Guyana Police Force, the Guyana Defence Force, the Maritime Administration Department, and other technical maritime experts,вҖқ Phillips said, adding that the accuracy of the passenger manifest and compliance with maritime safety regulations would be examined.

He added: вҖңLet me be absolutely clear: where negligence, misconduct, or criminal wrongdoing is established, those responsible will face the full force of the law..вҖқ

Earlier on Sunday, Phillips said the search and rescue coordination centre had expanded the search area from 400 sq km (154 sq miles) to 1,070 sq km, with additional aircraft and vessels deployed alongside local fisherfolk familiar with the waters.

As the rescue effort continued, survivors recounted the moments when the ferry overturned in darkness.

Adam Matabheek, who was travelling with his wife, brother, sister-in-law and three young nieces and nephews, said he noticed the vessel leaning several hours before it capsized.

вҖңWe dismissed it as rough water and went to sleep,вҖқ he said.

Moments later, the vessel rolled over. вҖңI ended up in the water,вҖқ he recalled, saying he managed to grab a lifejacket before drifting through the night.

His wife, Carlita Matabheek, who cannot swim, said she became trapped inside the overturned vessel before a lifejacket fell within her reach. вҖңI started to pray,вҖқ she said.

After calling repeatedly for her husband in the darkness, she felt someone tugging at her hair. вҖңIt was Adam,вҖқ she said.

The couple and AdamвҖҷs younger brother, Nathaniel, spent the night drifting in the sea until rescuers found them after daybreak.

вҖңGod cleared the skies and saved us,вҖқ Carlita said.

Their relief, however, was overshadowed by tragedy.

NathanielвҖҷs wife, Rachel Welch, and their three children вҖ“ Nathaniel Jr., 8, Natalia, 5, and Ezekiel, 3 вҖ“ remain among the missing. According to the family, Rachel was thrown from her hammock as the ferry capsized and tried desperately to reach her children before they disappeared into the water.

Another survivor, Loureen Singh, a trader who regularly travels to Port Kaituma to sell clothing, survived by holding on to the same lifejacket as Carlita after telling her she could swim вҖңa littleвҖқ.

Carlita urged her: вҖңHold on to me.вҖқ

The two women remained together throughout the night until they were rescued.

GuyanaвҖҷs international and regional partners have offered assistance in the search and rescue.

entries.title: Earthquake in Peru leaves at least six dead
entries.summary: More than 30 injured and 300 displaced after buildings collapse near SicayaA 5.5-magnitude earthquake shook the Andes region of Peru, killing at least six people, authorities said Sunday. More than 30 people were injured and 300 have been displaced.The US Geological Survey reported the quake struck on Saturday at 9.24pm, with its epicentre located 2km (1.2 miles) west-south-west of the city of Sic
entries.content:
A 5.5-magnitude earthquake shook the Andes region of Peru, killing at least six people, authorities said Sunday. More than 30 people were injured and 300 have been displaced.

The US Geological Survey reported the quake struck on Saturday at 9.24pm, with its epicentre located 2km (1.2 miles) west-south-west of the city of Sicaya in Huancayo province. It was 10km (6.2 miles) deep.

PeruвҖҷs National Civil Defence Institute said in a statement that the total number of missing people is still unknown. Several buildings collapsed or suffered structural damage, including the local church and convent.

Luis VГЎsquez, head of the local civil defence office, told journalists that the use of rustic adobe materials in construction in that Andean area вҖңhas contributed to the greater impact and damageвҖқ.

Earthquakes are frequent in Peru, which is located in the Pacific вҖңRing of FireвҖқ.

Images broadcast by local media captured the anguish of victimsвҖҷ relatives in one of the hardest-hit areas, the agricultural region of Chongo Bajo, where residents huddled under blankets outside severely damaged homes. Animals were also seen under the rubble.

вҖңMy home has been destroyed,вҖқ Hermenegilda Guamalato told a local radio station as she sought a place to stay with her three children in the neighbouring district of Huayucachi.

In 2007, a 7.9-magnitude earthquake struck the province of Pisco in the Ica region, killing nearly 600 people.
